walkthrough.title: Choose Your Roles
walkthrough.text: Let's get One Night Ultimate Werewolf set up! First, decide which roles to include. You always need exactly three more cards than the number of players. So if you have five players, grab eight role cards. For your first game, we recommend using two Werewolves, one Seer, one Robber, one Troublemaker, and one or two Villagers to fill out the rest. Keep it simple until everyone gets the hang of it.

walkthrough.title: Place the Role Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the matching role tokens for every card you picked. Place them face up in the center of the table so everyone can see exactly which roles are in the game. This is public information and it is super important. Knowing which roles are possible is the foundation of every argument and deduction during the day phase.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all the role cards you selected and deal one face down to each player. Place the remaining three cards face down in the center of the table. These are the center cards and they are a huge deal. One or more of the roles in play might actually be sitting in the center, which means no player has that role even though a token for it is showing.

walkthrough.title: Look at Your Card
walkthrough.text: Everyone picks up their card and secretly looks at it. Memorize your role, then place it face down in front of you. Do not look at it again for the rest of the game! Your card might get swapped during the night without you knowing. From this point forward, the card in front of you might not be the role you started with. Everyone ready? Close your eyes and let's begin the night phase.

summary.title: Choose Your Roles
bullets: Select role cards: always player count + 3
First game rec: 2 Werewolves, Seer, Robber, Troublemaker, fill with Villagers

summary.title: Place the Role Tokens
bullets: Place matching role tokens face up in center
Public info — everyone sees which roles are in the game

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Shuffle selected cards, deal 1 face-down to each player
Place remaining 3 cards face-down in center

summary.title: Look at Your Card
bullets: Secretly look at your role card, memorize it
Place face-down — do NOT look again
Your card may be swapped during the night without your knowledge

walkthrough.title: The Night Phase
walkthrough.text: Everyone closes their eyes. An announcer or the free companion app calls each role one at a time in a specific order. When your role is called, you open your eyes and perform your action silently. Then you close your eyes again. Roles that are not in the game are still called to keep things mysterious. The full wake order is Doppelganger, Werewolves, Minion, Masons, Seer, Robber, Troublemaker, Drunk, Insomniac.

walkthrough.title: Werewolf Action
walkthrough.text: When Werewolves are called, all Werewolf players open their eyes and look for each other. Now you know who your partner in crime is. If you are the only Werewolf who wakes up, that means the other Werewolf card is in the center. As a lone wolf, you get a bonus: you may peek at one of the three center cards. This gives you secret information to use or bluff with during the day.

walkthrough.title: Seer, Robber, and Troublemaker
walkthrough.text: The Seer wakes up and may look at one other player's card or two of the three center cards without moving them. The Robber may swap their card with another player's card and then look at the card they took. The Robber is now whatever role they stole and on that team. The Troublemaker may swap the cards of two other players without looking at either card. Those players are now different roles and they do not know it!

walkthrough.title: Other Night Roles
walkthrough.text: The Minion wakes up after Werewolves. The Werewolves stick their thumbs up so the Minion can see who they are, but the Werewolves do not learn who the Minion is. Masons wake up together and confirm each other. The Drunk must swap their card with one center card but does not get to look at it. The Insomniac wakes up last and peeks at their own card to see if it changed. Villagers, the Tanner, and the Hunter never wake up at night.

walkthrough.title: The Day Phase
walkthrough.text: Everyone opens their eyes and the real fun begins! You have a few minutes to discuss, argue, accuse, and try to figure out who the Werewolves are. Share what you learned during the night, or bluff about it. Werewolves will try to blend in and point suspicion elsewhere. Villagers need to piece together the clues and find contradictions. There is no set time limit, but five minutes of discussion works great for most groups.

walkthrough.title: The Vote
walkthrough.text: When discussion is over, everyone counts to three and simultaneously points at one other player. You must point at someone, and you cannot point at yourself. The player with the most votes is eliminated. If two or more players are tied for the most votes, all tied players are eliminated. If every single player receives exactly one vote, nobody dies.

walkthrough.title: The Hunter's Chain Kill
walkthrough.text: If the Hunter is eliminated, the player the Hunter is pointing at also dies, regardless of how many votes that second player received. This can completely change the outcome, so pay attention to where the Hunter is pointing before the vote. The Hunter's ability is based on their final role card, not what they started as.

walkthrough.title: Who Wins?
walkthrough.text: Now flip all the cards face up and check everyone's final role. If at least one Werewolf player is eliminated, the village team wins. If no Werewolves are eliminated, the Werewolf team wins, including the Minion. There is a special case: if both Werewolf cards are in the center, the village team wins only if nobody dies. The Tanner is a wildcard who wins only if the Tanner is eliminated, and the Tanner's death blocks the Werewolf team from winning.

summary.title: The Night Phase
bullets: All eyes closed; announcer/app calls roles in order
Wake order: Doppelganger → Werewolves → Minion → Masons → Seer → Robber → Troublemaker → Drunk → Insomniac
Roles not in game are still called for cover

bullets: Werewolves open eyes, identify each other
Lone wolf: may peek at 1 center card
Werewolves are on the werewolf team

summary.title: Seer, Robber, and Troublemaker
bullets: Seer: look at 1 player's card OR 2 center cards (no moving)
Robber: swap your card with another player's, look at new card (join that team)
Troublemaker: swap 2 other players' cards without looking

summary.title: Other Night Roles
bullets: Minion: sees Werewolves (thumbs up), but Werewolves don't learn Minion
Masons: wake together, confirm each other
Drunk: swap card with 1 center card, don't look
Insomniac: check own card to see if it changed
Villager, Tanner, Hunter: never wake

bullets: Everyone opens eyes — discuss, accuse, bluff
Share or hide night info, find contradictions
~5 min discussion recommended

bullets: Count to 3, everyone points at another player simultaneously
Most votes → eliminated; tied → all tied players eliminated
All get exactly 1 vote → nobody dies

Based on final role card, not starting role
Can change the game outcome dramatically

summary.title: Who Wins?
bullets: Village wins: at least 1 Werewolf player dies
Werewolf team wins: no Werewolves die (includes Minion)
Both Werewolves in center: village wins only if nobody dies
Tanner wins only by dying; Tanner death blocks Werewolf win

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through a complete round with six example players using seven roles: two Werewolves, one Seer, one Robber, one Troublemaker, one Villager, one Insomniac, and three center cards. Follow along even if your group is a different size. Let's see some deception in action!

walkthrough.title: The Deal
walkthrough.text: Cards are shuffled and dealt. Here is what everyone secretly sees: Player one is a Werewolf. Player two is the Seer. Player three is the Robber. Player four is the Troublemaker. Player five is a Villager. Player six is the Insomniac. The second Werewolf card is sitting in the center along with two other cards. Everyone memorizes their role and places their card face down.

walkthrough.title: Night Phase: Werewolves Wake
walkthrough.text: Eyes closed, everyone. Werewolves, wake up! Player one opens their eyes and looks around, but nobody else opens their eyes. Player one is a lone wolf! Because they are the only Werewolf, they get to peek at one center card. They see a Villager in the center. Now Player one knows the second Werewolf card is also in the center somewhere. Werewolves, close your eyes.

walkthrough.title: Night Phase: Info Roles Wake
walkthrough.text: Seer, wake up! Player two looks at Player five's card and sees Villager. Good to know. Robber, wake up! Player three swaps their card with Player one's card. Player three looks at the card they just took and sees Werewolf. Player three is now a Werewolf and Player one is now the Robber, but Player one has no idea this happened!

walkthrough.title: Night Phase: More Actions
walkthrough.text: Troublemaker, wake up! Player four swaps the cards of Player two and Player five without looking. Player two is now the Villager and Player five is now the Seer, but neither of them knows it. Insomniac, wake up! Player six peeks at their own card and sees it is still Insomniac. Nobody swapped them. Night is over, everyone open your eyes!

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Tunnel Vision
walkthrough.text: Discussion begins! Player two confidently says they are the Seer and checked Player five, who is a Villager. Player five confirms, saying they looked at their card and saw Villager. But here is the problem: Player two does not realize their card was swapped by the Troublemaker. Player two is no longer the Seer. They are now the Villager. And Player five is now the Seer. Neither of them is lying, but both are wrong about their current roles!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Your Role Can Change
walkthrough.text: This is the most important lesson in One Night. Your starting role is not necessarily your final role. The Robber and Troublemaker can change everything. When someone claims a role, ask yourself: could their card have been swapped after they saw it? Always consider the Troublemaker and Robber as potential game-changers before trusting any claim at face value.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: The Werewolf Panics
walkthrough.text: Player one remembers being the Werewolf and panics. They stay quiet and hope nobody notices them. When someone asks Player one what their role is, they mumble something vague like "I think I am just a Villager." But they do not commit to any details. The hesitation is a huge red flag and draws immediate suspicion from the group.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Have a Claim Ready
walkthrough.text: Whether you are a Villager or a Werewolf, you need a confident claim. Hesitation looks guilty even when you are innocent. Before discussion starts, decide what you are going to say. If you are a Werewolf, pick a role to fake and have details ready. If you are a Villager, state it clearly and help drive the conversation. The worst thing you can do is sit there with nothing to say.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Robber
walkthrough.text: Player three, who robbed Player one and is now actually the Werewolf, claims to be the Robber who stole from Player one and saw a Villager card. This is a bold lie! The group focuses on Player one's weak claim and ignores the fact that Player three's Robber claim means Player one should now be the Robber, not a Villager. Nobody catches the contradiction.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track the Swap Chain
walkthrough.text: Every swap creates a chain of logic. If someone claims Robber and says they stole from Player X, then Player X should now be the Robber. If Player X claims something else, one of them is lying. Draw the swap chain in your head: who moved what, and does it all add up? The swaps are your best tool for finding contradictions, so never ignore them.

walkthrough.title: Resolution
walkthrough.text: Let's say the group votes and eliminates Player one. Cards flip over. Player one's card is actually the Robber now, because Player three swapped with them. Player three's card is the Werewolf. The village accidentally killed an innocent Robber! Since no actual Werewolf was eliminated, the Werewolf team wins. If the group had tracked the swap chain carefully, they would have caught Player three's lie.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into your first real game, here are three things that will help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on bluffing, claiming, and reading other players.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick breakdown of every role and how they interact.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun and trust nobody!

summary.title: Welcome to Your First Game
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 6 players and 7 roles
Roles: 2 Werewolves, Seer, Robber, Troublemaker, Villager, Insomniac + 3 center cards
Follow along even with a different group size

Insomniac (P6) checks own card — still Insomniac, no swap

Hesitation draws immediate suspicion

summary.title: Lesson: Always Have a Claim Ready
bullets: Confident claim needed whether village or werewolf
Werewolves: pick a fake role and prepare details
Villagers: state clearly and help drive conversation

summary.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Robber
bullets: P3 (now Werewolf) claims Robber, says they saw Villager from P1
Group ignores that P1 should now be Robber if P3's claim is true
Nobody catches the contradiction

summary.title: Lesson: Track the Swap Chain
bullets: Every swap creates a chain of logic — trace it
If Robber stole from X, then X should now be Robber
Contradictions in swap chains reveal liars

P3's card is the Werewolf — no Werewolf died

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → bluffing, claiming, and reading players
Reference the Appendix → quick breakdown of every role
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, treating your starting role as your final role. Swaps change everything, so always think about what your card is now, not what it was. Second, staying silent when you have nothing to hide. Quiet players look guilty. Third, ignoring the center cards. If both Werewolves could be in the center, killing someone hands the Werewolves a free win. Fourth, revealing information too early without getting anything back. Information is currency in this game.

walkthrough.title: How to Claim as Village Team
walkthrough.text: If you are on the village team, your job is to share useful information and build trust. Information roles like the Seer and Robber should usually claim early because their info helps the village coordinate votes. Masons should confirm each other immediately since a verified pair is incredibly powerful. The Insomniac should share whether their card changed, which can confirm or deny Troublemaker and Robber claims. Even Villagers should speak up and contribute to the discussion by asking pointed questions.

walkthrough.title: How to Bluff as Werewolf Team
walkthrough.text: If you are a Werewolf, you need to blend in. The safest bluff is usually claiming Villager because there are no details to get caught on. But in games with multiple Villagers, a more aggressive bluff can be stronger. Claiming Seer and naming someone as a Werewolf puts them on the defensive. Claiming Robber and saying you stole a Villager card explains your knowledge while deflecting suspicion. Whatever you claim, commit fully and do not change your story.

walkthrough.title: Reading Other Players
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to behavior more than words. Watch for hesitation when someone is asked a direct question. Notice who jumps to accuse others before sharing their own information. Look for over-explaining, which often signals a lie. And track who stays quiet during key moments. In this game, everyone has a role and should have something to say. If someone seems like they are stalling for time, push them for specifics.

walkthrough.title: The Center Card Trap
walkthrough.text: One of the trickiest situations is when both Werewolf cards might be in the center. If that is the case, the village team wins by making sure nobody gets eliminated. But if a real Werewolf is hiding among the players, voting to kill nobody hands them the win. Use information roles to check center cards whenever possible. The Seer can look at two center cards, and the lone Werewolf peeks at one. These pieces of information are critical for figuring out whether to vote or hold fire.

walkthrough.title: Vote Strategy
walkthrough.text: The vote is the most important moment in the game. Before voting, check if anyone has received enough suspicion to be a clear target. If the group is split, try to build consensus around one player. If you are a Werewolf, try to split the vote so that nobody gets a majority, or point the group toward an innocent player. Remember the special tie rule: if everyone gets exactly one vote, nobody dies. Werewolves can try to engineer this by spreading votes thin.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Information
walkthrough.text: When you reveal information matters just as much as what you reveal. If you claim Seer immediately, Werewolves can build their lies around your info. But if you wait too long, the group might not have enough time to process it. A strong middle ground is to let one or two players talk first, then reveal your information and see if it contradicts anything. This catches liars off guard because they already committed to a story before you dropped your knowledge.

walkthrough.title: The Tanner Wildcard
walkthrough.text: The Tanner wants to die. They will act suspicious on purpose, make wild accusations, and generally try to get voted out. The danger is that if the Tanner dies and no Werewolf dies, the Werewolf team does not win but the village team also loses. If you suspect someone might be the Tanner, do not vote for them unless you can also eliminate a Werewolf. A Tanner playing well will look exactly like a bad Werewolf, so be cautious with easy targets.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Starting role ≠ final role — swaps change everything
Silence looks guilty even when you're innocent
Ignoring center cards can hand werewolves a free win
Don't reveal info without getting something back

Masons: confirm each other immediately for a verified pair
Insomniac: share whether card changed to verify other claims
Villagers: ask pointed questions, stay engaged

summary.title: How to Bluff as Werewolf Team
bullets: Safest bluff: Villager (no details to contradict)
Aggressive bluff: fake Seer and name someone as Werewolf
Robber bluff: claim you stole a safe card
Commit fully — never change your story

summary.title: Reading Other Players
bullets: Watch for hesitation on direct questions
Notice who accuses before sharing their own info
Over-explaining often signals a lie
Push quiet players for specifics

summary.title: The Center Card Trap
bullets: Both Werewolves in center → village wins only if nobody dies
Seer can check 2 center cards to verify
Lone wolf peeks at 1 center card — track this info
Killing someone when Werewolves are in center = village loses

summary.title: Vote Strategy
bullets: Build consensus around one target before voting
Werewolves: split the vote or redirect to innocents
All players get exactly 1 vote → nobody dies
Ties eliminate ALL tied players

summary.title: Timing Your Information
bullets: Too early: Werewolves build lies around your info
Too late: group can't process it
Best: let 1-2 players talk first, then reveal to catch contradictions

summary.title: The Tanner Wildcard
bullets: Tanner wins only by dying — acts suspiciously on purpose
Tanner death blocks Werewolf win but village also loses
Don't vote for easy targets without checking for Tanner
Good Tanner looks exactly like a bad Werewolf

walkthrough.title: Werewolf Team Coordination
walkthrough.text: When two Werewolves wake up together, you have a brief but powerful moment. Make eye contact and remember each other. During the day, your goal is to corroborate each other's claims without being obvious about it. If one Werewolf claims Seer and names an innocent player as a Werewolf, the other Werewolf should subtly support the accusation without immediately piling on. Too much agreement too fast looks coordinated.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Lone Wolf Play
walkthrough.text: Being a lone Werewolf is actually an advantage in some ways. You peeked at a center card, which gives you real information to weave into a bluff. If you saw the Seer in the center, you know nobody at the table is actually the Seer, so you can safely claim Seer yourself. If you saw a Villager in the center, you can claim Robber and say you stole that Villager from another player. Use your center card knowledge to build an airtight story.

walkthrough.title: Minion Sacrifice Play
walkthrough.text: The Minion knows who the Werewolves are but the Werewolves do not know the Minion. This creates a powerful sacrifice strategy. If the Minion draws all the suspicion and gets voted out, the Werewolf team still wins because no Werewolf died. The Minion can act extremely suspicious on purpose, make contradictory claims, and basically become a lightning rod. Even better, the Minion can claim to be a Werewolf to bait the village into voting for them instead of the real Werewolf.

walkthrough.title: Seer Confirmation Techniques
walkthrough.text: As the Seer, choosing what to look at is your biggest decision. Looking at one player's card gives you a confirmed ally or a confirmed Werewolf. Looking at two center cards tells you which roles are not in play, which helps you verify or debunk other claims. In games with lots of action roles, center checks are often more powerful because they eliminate possibilities. In games with lots of Villagers, checking a player is usually better because you need to find the hidden Werewolf.

walkthrough.title: Troublemaker Power Plays
walkthrough.text: The Troublemaker is one of the most disruptive roles in the game. The strongest move is often to swap two players who you think are on the same team. If you swap two suspected Werewolves, one of them is now on the village team and has an incentive to turn on the other. If you swap a confirmed Villager with a suspected Werewolf, the Villager is now the Werewolf and might get voted out by accident. Always announce who you swapped so the village can reason about the new roles.

walkthrough.title: Robber Decision Matrix
walkthrough.text: The Robber has a unique choice: steal from someone or stay put. If you suspect someone might be a Werewolf, stealing from them is incredibly strong because you learn the truth and also change your own team. If the card is a Werewolf, you are now on the Werewolf team and your goal flips completely. You need to protect yourself and avoid getting caught. If it is not a Werewolf, you have confirmed an ally. Either way, information is power.

walkthrough.title: Engineering the Vote
walkthrough.text: Advanced play is all about controlling the final vote. Werewolves want to either split votes evenly so nobody dies, or pile votes onto an innocent player. Village team wants consensus on a single target. The key technique is commitment: get players to publicly state their vote target before the count. Once someone commits out loud, it is harder for them to switch. Werewolves can exploit this by committing early to an innocent target and creating momentum.

walkthrough.title: Meta-Gaming and Player Reads
walkthrough.text: After several games with the same group, you start to learn their tells. Some people always fidget when they lie. Some people get aggressive when they are Werewolves. Some people go quiet when they have important information. These meta-reads become more powerful than any in-game logic. But be careful: experienced players know their own tells and will deliberately reverse them. The best players vary their behavior from game to game so they cannot be read easily.

summary.title: Werewolf Team Coordination
bullets: Make eye contact during night — remember your partner
Corroborate claims subtly, don't pile on too fast
Too much agreement too quickly looks coordinated

summary.title: Advanced Lone Wolf Play
bullets: Center card peek = real info for building airtight bluffs
Saw Seer in center → safely claim Seer
Saw Villager → claim Robber, say you stole it from someone

summary.title: Minion Sacrifice Play
bullets: Minion knows Werewolves but not vice versa
Minion dying = Werewolf team still wins
Act suspicious on purpose to draw all the votes
Can even claim Werewolf to bait village

summary.title: Seer Confirmation Techniques
bullets: 1 player card: confirmed ally or confirmed Werewolf
2 center cards: eliminate possibilities, debunk claims
Many action roles → center check stronger
Many Villagers → player check stronger

summary.title: Troublemaker Power Plays
bullets: Swap 2 suspected same-team players to flip allegiances
Swapping confirmed Villager with suspect → risky but powerful
Always announce who you swapped for village reasoning

summary.title: Robber Decision Matrix
bullets: Steal from suspected Werewolf: learn truth + possibly flip teams
If stolen card is Werewolf: you're now werewolf team, protect yourself
If not Werewolf: confirmed ally
Either way, information is power

summary.title: Engineering the Vote
bullets: Werewolves: split votes evenly or pile onto innocent
Village: build consensus on single target
Get public commitments before count — harder to switch
Werewolves commit early to create momentum toward innocents

summary.title: Meta-Gaming and Player Reads
bullets: Learn group tells: fidgeting, aggression, silence patterns
Meta-reads can be stronger than in-game logic
Experienced players reverse their own tells deliberately
Best players vary behavior game to game

entries.definition: Werewolf team. Wakes at night to identify other Werewolves. If you are the only Werewolf awake, you may peek at one center card. Your goal is to avoid being eliminated during the vote.
Werewolf team. Wakes after Werewolves and sees who they are (they put thumbs up). Werewolves do not learn who the Minion is. If Minion dies and no Werewolf dies, the Werewolf team still wins.
Village team. Wakes at night and may look at one other player's card OR two center cards without moving them. Provides critical information to the village during the day phase.

Village team (initially). Wakes at night and may swap their card with another player's card, then looks at the new card. The Robber is now on the team of the stolen role. If they choose not to rob, they remain on the village team.
Village team. Wakes at night and may swap the cards of two other players without looking at them. Those players are now different roles and do not know it. A powerful disruptive ability.

Village team. Both Mason cards must be included if Masons are in the game. Masons wake at night and confirm each other. If only one Mason wakes, the other Mason card is in the center. Provides a verified trust pair.
Village team (initially). Must swap their card with one center card without looking at it. The Drunk is now whatever role they blindly grabbed from the center, but does not know what it is.
Village team. Wakes up last during the night and looks at their own card to see if it was changed by the Robber or Troublemaker. Best used when Robber and/or Troublemaker are in the game.
Village team. Has no night action and never wakes up. Contributes during the day by asking questions, analyzing claims, and voting. Up to three Villager cards can be in the game.
Village team. Never wakes at night. If the Hunter is eliminated, the player the Hunter is pointing at also dies regardless of vote count. This chain kill can dramatically change the outcome.
Independent. Never wakes at night. The Tanner wins only if the Tanner is eliminated. If Tanner dies and no Werewolf dies, the Werewolf team does NOT win. Not on either team.

Varies. Wakes first and looks at one other player's card without swapping. Becomes that role and joins that team immediately. If the copied role has a night action, the Doppelganger performs it right away or later depending on the role.

The first phase of the game where all players close their eyes. Roles are called one at a time in a fixed order. Each role performs its action silently, then closes eyes again. All swaps and information gathering happen during this phase.
The second phase where all players open their eyes and discuss. Players share information, bluff, accuse, and try to identify the Werewolves. Typically lasts about five minutes before the vote.

The final moment of the game. On the count of three, all players simultaneously point at one other player. The player with the most votes is eliminated. Ties eliminate all tied players. If everyone gets exactly one vote, nobody dies.

The three face-down cards placed in the middle of the table during setup. These are roles that no player has. Some night actions interact with center cards (Seer can peek, Drunk swaps, lone Werewolf peeks). Center cards can dramatically change game dynamics.

The role card physically in front of a player after all night swaps are complete. This determines your team and win condition, NOT the role you started with. Robber, Troublemaker, and Drunk can all change your final role.
The sequence of card movements caused by the Robber, Troublemaker, and Drunk during the night. Tracking the swap chain is essential for deducing everyone's final roles during the day discussion.

The fixed sequence in which roles are called during the night: Doppelganger, Werewolves, Minion, Masons, Seer, Robber, Troublemaker, Drunk, Insomniac, Doppelganger-Insomniac. Roles not in the game are still called.
Tokens placed face up in the center of the table showing which roles are in the current game. This is public information that all players use to reason about possible claims and deductions.
